* You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting,

* You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a 
bowel movement.
* Your pain is not improving within ___ hours or not gone

Output:
answer:
The patient is a 47-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with severe abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She had a history of colon adenocarcinoma and had undergone a low anterior resection 7 months prior. She presented with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain, which was similar to a previous small bowel obstruction (SBO) she experienced after her colectomy. 

The patient's past medical history includes breast cancer, cervical DDD, and depression. She has no known family history of breast or ovarian cancer, but her maternal grandmother had colon cancer. 

The patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy and lysis of adhesions, and was found to have a strangulating small bowel obstruction. She was managed with IV fluids, medications, and a nasogastric tube, and was eventually able to tolerate a regular diet. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of strangulating small bowel obstruction, and was prescribed medications including venlafaxine, famotidine, docusate sodium, and hydromorphone. She was advised to follow up with her doctor in 6 weeks and to avoid lifting heavy objects, driving, or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications.

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery
Lower Extremity Bypass Surgery Discharge Instructions

What to expect when you go home:
1. It is normal to feel tired, this will last for ___ weeks 
You should get up out of bed every day and gradually increase 
your activity each day 
Unless you were told not to bear any weight on operative foot: 
you may walk and you may go up and down stairs 
Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too 
much right away!

2. It is normal to have swelling of the leg you were operated 
on:
Elevate your leg above the level of your heart (use ___ 
pillows or a recliner) every ___ hours throughout the day and at 
night
Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your 
legs elevated
        You may use a light ACE wrap for slight compression of 
your leg.  Be careful not to apply this tightly.

3. It is normal to have a decreased appetite, your appetite will 
return with time 
You will probably lose your taste for food and lose some 
weight 
Eat small frequent meals
It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber, 
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to 
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool 
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
No driving until post-op visit and you are no longer taking 
pain medications
Unless you were told not to bear any weight on operative foot: 

You should get up every day, get dressed and walk
You should gradually increase your activity
You may up and down stairs, go outside and/or ride in a car
Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too 
much right away!
No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 pounds) 
until your post op visit 
You may shower (unless you have stitches or foot incisions) no 
direct spray on incision, let the soapy water run over incision, 
rinse and pat dry
Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small 
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing 
over the area that is draining, as needed
Take all the medications you were taking before surgery, 
unless otherwise directed
Take one full strength (325mg) enteric coated aspirin daily, 
unless otherwise directed 
Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in 2 weeks for 
staple/suture removal

What to report to office:
Redness that extends away from your incision
A sudden increase in pain that is not controlled with pain 
medication
A sudden change in the ability to move or use your leg or the 
ability to feel your leg
Temperature greater than 100.5F for 24 hours
Bleeding, new or increased drainage from incision or white, 
yellow or green drainage from incisions

*Take coumadin daily as instructed by your PCP.  You will need 
to have daily INR checks with the visiting nurse or the 
outpatient lab until you are stabilized on a regimen.*

___

answer:
The patient is a 63-year-old male who was admitted for a planned right lower extremity angiography to evaluate his nonhealing right foot wound. He has a history of bilateral lower extremity bypass grafts, IDDM, HTN, GERD, CVA, CAD, and CHF. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His presenting symptoms included a 1-week history of cellulitis of his right toe, and he was being treated for a nonhealing ulcer on his third toe. He also experienced vertigo and had a fall while getting out of bed, resulting in a small laceration around his right eye.

The patient underwent right lower extremity angiography, which revealed occlusion distal to the existing right bypass, and he was scheduled for revision of his bypass graft. He also underwent right femoral to posterior tibial artery bypass with PTFE without complication. He was treated with medications including aspirin, meclizine, oxycodone, Bactrim DS, NPH insulin, and Coumadin.

At discharge, the patient was cleared to go home with daily physical therapy and visiting nurse care. He was advised to follow up with his PCP in 2 weeks for INR check and Coumadin dosing, and to address his hypertension for optimal long-term management.

Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Cerebral aneurysm

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Elective Aneurysm Clipping

Call your neurosurgeons office and speak to the Nurse 
Practitioner if you experience:

Any neurological issues, such as change in vision, speech or 
movement

Swelling, drainage, or redness of your incision

Any problems with medications, such as nausea vomiting or 
lethargy

Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit

Headaches not relieved with prescribed medications

Activity:

Start to resume all activities as you tolerate  but start 
slowly and increase at your own pace.

Do not operate any motorized vehicle for at least 10 days after 
your surgery  your Nurse Practitioner can give you more detail 
at the time of your suture removal.  

Incision Care:

Keep your wound clean and dry.

Do not use shampoo until your sutures are removed. 

When you are allowed to shampoo your hair, let the shampoo run 
off the incision line.  Gently pad the incision with a towel to 
dry.

Do not rub, scrub, scratch, or pick at any scabs on the incision 
line.

You need your sutures removed 7 to 10 days after surgery

Post-Operative Experiences: Physical

Jaw pain on the same side as your surgery; this goes away after 
about a month

You may experience constipation.  Constipation can be prevented 
by:

oDrinking plenty of fluids
oIncreasing fiber in your diet by eating vegetables, prunes, 
fiber rich breads and cereals, or fiber supplements
oExercising
oUsing over-the-counter bowel stimulants or laxatives as 
needed, stopping usage if you experience loose bowel movements 
or diarrhea

Fatigue which will slowly resolve over time

Numbness or tingling in the area of the incision; this can take 
weeks or months to fully resolve

Muffled hearing in the ear near the incision area

Low back pain or shooting pain down the leg which can resolve 
with increased activity

Post-Operative Experiences: Emotional

You may experience depression.  Symptoms of depression can 
include

oFeeling down or sad
oIrritability, frustration, and confusion
oDistractibility
oLower Self-Esteem/Relationship Challenges
oInsomnia
oLoneliness

If you experience these symptoms, you can contact your Primary 
Care Provider who can make a referral to a Psychologist or 
Psychiatrist

You can also seek out a local Brain Aneurysm Support Group in 
your area through the Brain Aneurysm Foundation

oMore information can be found at ___

 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:
answer: The patient is a 54-year-old male who was admitted for an elective left pterional craniotomy and clipping of a left ophthalmic artery aneurysm. He had a history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, thyroid surgery, bursitis, kidney stones, and a stroke. He was allergic to sea conch, IV contrast, and oxycodone. The patient's chief complaint was elective aneurysm clipping, and he underwent a successful surgery. He was monitored in the ICU for 24 hours before being transferred to the general floors. On discharge, the patient was noted to have L nasolabial flattening, but was otherwise intact. He was prescribed medications including acetaminophen-caff-butalbital, docusate sodium, lisinopril, and simvastatin. The patient was discharged in stable condition and instructed to follow up with his neurosurgeon's office and to resume activities slowly.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
 
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 
 
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
 
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
 
6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc).
 
8. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your Lovenox for four (4) 
weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  If 
you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose while taking anticoagulation 
medication.  ___ STOCKINGS x 6 WEEKS.
 
9. WOUND CARE: Please keep your Silverlon dressing clean and 
dry. Remove on ___. It is okay to shower five days after 
surgery but no tub baths, swimming, or submerging your incision 
until after your four (4) week checkup. You may place a dry 
sterile dressing on the wound after the Silverlon dressing is 
removed, otherwise leave it open to air. Check wound regularly 
for signs of infection such as redness or thick yellow drainage. 
Staples will be removed by the visiting nurse or rehab facility 
in two (2) weeks.
 
10. ___ (once at home): Home ___, dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks, and staple removal at two weeks after 
surgery.
 
11. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated with walker or 2 
crutches at all times for six weeks.  Posterior precautions. No 
strenuous exercise or heavy lifting until follow up appointment. 
 Mobilize frequently.

ice

answer:
The patient is a 55-year-old female who was admitted to the orthopedic surgery service for a right hip replacement due to refractory right hip osteoarthritis. She has a past medical history of hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis. She is allergic to codeine and gluten. The patient's chief complaint was right hip pain, which was well-controlled during her hospital stay. She underwent a successful right total hip replacement procedure and was discharged to home with services in stable condition.

The patient's medications on admission included dextroamphetamine-amphetamine, butalbital-acetaminophen-caff, lidocaine, estrogens conjugated, multivitamins, lutein, melatonin, ibuprofen, trazodone, levothyroxine sodium, and ascorbic acid. At discharge, she was prescribed dextroamphetamine-amphetamine, levothyroxine sodium, multivitamins, trazodone, vitamin D, acetaminophen, docusate sodium, enoxaparin sodium, oxycoDONE, senna, ascorbic acid, and butalbital-acetaminophen-caff.

The patient was advised to follow up with her primary physician regarding her admission and new medications, to resume her home medications unless otherwise instructed, and to take her pain medications as directed. She was also instructed to avoid driving, operating heavy machinery, or drinking alcohol while taking her medications, and to call her surgeon's office to schedule or confirm her follow-up appointment.

admitted to the hospital with complaints of chest pain and were 
having a heart attack. You were immediately taken to the cardiac 
catheterization lab where they identifified a blockage 
consistnet with the changes on your EKG which they thought to be 
the cause of your acute heart attack. This vessel is called the 
right coroanry artery. This artery was stented with a drug 
coated stent. At that time it was also confirmed by visualizing 
all of the arteries that feed the heart muscle that you have 
blockages in the other arteries of your heart. 

You have also been identified as having a blcokage in your right 
carotid artery.

Given these issues we are sending you home with tight medication 
control as detailed below and with a plan to discuss the most 
appropriate interventions for your coronary artery disease as 
well as your carotid blockage.

area the stent was placed, DO NOT stop this medication unless 
specifically spoken to by Dr. ___.

cholesterol management
Metorpolol 75mg XL daily: This is to decrease your heart rate 
and thereby the workload of the heart.
Nitroglycerin tablets: placed under the tongue and dissolve if 
you experience chest pain or shortness of breath this medicaiton 
acutely dilates the arteries that feed the heart muscle. You can 
take one every five minutes for a total of three and shoudl call 
___ if you do not experience relief. 

Additionally while you were in the hospital you received two 
large doses of contrast which can effect your kidneys. You 
should have started a drug called an ACE inhibitor as part of 
your recovery after your heart attack, but this can also cause 
some damage to vulnerable kidneys so we are holding this drug 
for now. This should be reconsidered once your labs are back to 
baseline and we know your kidney function is back to baseline.

He has a history of hyperlipidemia and has had one week of exertional left arm pain associated with nausea. He was found to have inferior STEMI and was sent to the cath lab for further evaluation. The patient underwent a left heart cardiac catheterization with a drug-coated stent placed in the right coronary artery. He was also noted to have severe 3-vessel disease.

The patient's past medical history includes dyslipidemia, GERD, gastric polyps, and diverticulosis. He has no history of diabetes, hypertension, or cardiac surgery. He is allergic to Zocor and Lipitor.

The patient's diagnosis is high-grade stenosis of the right internal carotid artery at its origin, and regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction suggestive of multivessel ischemia.

The patient underwent the following procedures: left heart cardiac catheterization with a drug-coated stent placed in the right coronary artery.

The patient was prescribed the following medications at discharge: aspirin 325 daily, Plavix 75mg daily, metoprolol 37.5 BID, captopril 6.25mg BID, and rosuvastatin 20mg daily.

The patient will require both CABG and CEA in the coming weeks, and the cardiac surgery and vascular surgery teams will discuss the approach in terms of performing interval vs. simultaneous procedures.

Output:
answer: The patient is a 65-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with hypoxic respiratory failure. She has a past medical history of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), orthostatic hypotension, and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). She has allergies to penicillins and oxybutynin. The patient's chief complaint was hypoxia, dyspnea, productive cough, and congestion, which she experienced for the past two days. She was previously intubated and had a tracheostomy and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) placement. She was referred to the emergency department for stoma closure and was found to have tachypnea, rhonchorous lungs, and a stoma site. The patient was given various medications, including albuterol, ipratropium bromide, and cefepime. She was also given oxygen therapy and had a flutter valve inserted. The patient's physical examination revealed clear lungs, regular heart rate and rhythm, and normal abdominal examination. The patient's laboratory results showed anemia, leukocytosis, and elevated B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP). Imaging studies revealed extensive atelectasis in the lower lungs, which could not exclude a superimposed pneumonia. The patient was discharged with a tracheostomy mask and speaking valve, and was advised to use a flutter valve and oxygen therapy.

answer:
The patient is a 58-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital for surgical correction of an underling deformity in her right foot. She has a history of diabetes, depression, and hypertension. She is allergic to penicillins and bee pollens. Her chief complaint was a foot ulcer and hammer toes in her right foot. She underwent flexor tenotomies and metatarsal head resection.

The patient's physical examination revealed afebrile and stable vital signs, with palpable pedal pulses and normal sensation and proprioception in her extremities. Her ulceration was described as a partial thickness sub-metatarsal head ulcer with a granular base and regular margins.

The patient's discharge medications included bisacodyl, vitamin E, multivitamin, citalopram, byetta, albuterol, lisinopril, oxybutynin, simvastatin, spironolactone, fluticasone, metformin, clonazepam, trazodone, linezolid, acetaminophen, and docusate sodium. She was also prescribed a wheelchair with removable arm rests and elevating leg lifts.

The patient was discharged in good condition with instructions to resume all pre-admission medications, keep her dressing clean and dry, and remain non-weight bearing on her right foot in a surgical shoe with crutches at all times.

#MCA and ACOM aneurysms

Post operatively, the patient had ongoing nausea and multiple

medications were increased, with concern for undertreated pain

Discharge Instructions

Dr. ___ & Dr. ___

___ Aneurysm ___

Call your neurosurgeons office and speak to the Nurse 
Practitioner if you experience:

-Any neurological issues, such as change in vision, speech or 
movement

-Swelling, drainage, or redness of your incision

-Any problems with medications, such as nausea vomiting or 
lethargy

-Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit

-Headaches not relieved with prescribed medications

Activity:

-Start to resume all activities as you tolerate  but start 
slowly and increase at your own pace.

-Do not operate any motorized vehicle for at least 10 days 
after your surgery  your Nurse Practitioner can give you more 
detail at the time of your suture removal.  

Incision Care:

-Keep your wound clean and dry.

-Do not use shampoo until your sutures are removed. 

-When you are allowed to shampoo your hair, let the shampoo run 
off the incision line.  Gently pad the incision with a towel to 
dry.

-Do not rub, scrub, scratch, or pick at any scabs on the 
incision line.

-You need your sutures removed 7 to 10 days after surgery

Post-Operative Experiences: Physical

-Jaw pain on the same side as your surgery; this goes away 
after about a month

-You may experience constipation.  Constipation can be 
prevented by:

oDrinking plenty of fluids
oIncreasing fiber in your diet by eating vegetables, prunes, 
fiber rich breads and cereals, or fiber supplements
oExercising
oUsing over-the-counter bowel stimulants or laxatives as 
needed, stopping usage if you experience loose bowel movements 
or diarrhea

-Fatigue which will slowly resolve over time

-Numbness or tingling in the area of the incision; this can 
take weeks or months to fully resolve

-Muffled hearing in the ear near the incision area

-Low back pain or shooting pain down the leg which can resolve 
with increased activity

Post-Operative Experiences: Emotional

-You may experience depression.  Symptoms of depression can 
include

oFeeling down or sad
oIrritability, frustration, and confusion
oDistractibility
oLower Self-Esteem/Relationship Challenges
oInsomnia
oLoneliness

-If you experience these symptoms, you can contact your Primary 
Care Provider who can make a referral to a Psychologist or 
Psychiatrist

-You can also seek out a local Brain Aneurysm Support Group in 
your area through the Brain Aneurysm Foundation

oMore information can be found at ___

 
Followup Instructions:
___

He has a history of methadone dependence, polycystic kidney disease, hepatitis C, iron deficiency anemia, and a cardiac heart murmur. He also has a family history of adult polycystic kidney disease and an aneurysm that was previously fixed.

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced nausea and vomiting, which was treated with medication and an NG tube. He also had hypertension, which was managed with medication, and urinary retention, which was treated with a foley catheter and straight catheterization.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of right MCA and AComm aneurysm and was prescribed medications including acetaminophen, senna, albuterol, clonidine, lisinopril, methadone, and pantoprazole. He was advised to follow up with his neurosurgeon's office and to contact his nurse practitioner if he experiences any neurological issues, swelling, drainage, or redness of his incision, or any problems with medications.

The patient was also given discharge instructions to start resuming activities slowly, to avoid operating any motorized vehicles for at least 10 days after surgery, and to keep his wound clean and dry. He was also advised to follow up with his primary care provider if he experiences depression or any other emotional symptoms.

She has a history of locally advanced pancreatic cancer on palliative chemotherapy and was recently admitted to another hospital for cellulitis of the right lower extremity. She completed her antibiotics as an outpatient and had clinical improvement with improvement in functional status. The patient started on paclitaxel/gemcitabine on a previous day and had an episode of diarrhea, which resolved with Imodium. She was found to have a white blood cell count of 18k and was advised to come to the emergency department.

The patient's past medical history includes pancreatic cancer stage III, depression, gastroesophageal reflux, and skin cancers. She has a family history of myocardial infarction in her mother and sister, as well as a brother with an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator and a history of arrhythmia.

The patient's physical examination revealed a pleasant and comfortable patient with no significant rashes or ecchymoses. Her laboratory results showed mild elevation in alkaline phosphatase and a white blood cell count of 17.9. Imaging studies showed a known pancreatic head mass and cholelithiasis without sonographic evidence of acute cholecystitis.

The patient was diagnosed with fever and was treated with Vanc/Cefepime. She was also prescribed medications at discharge, including Vanc/Cefepime and antibiotics.

The patient is an 84-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital with a chief complaint of chest pain. She has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and mild aortic stenosis. She presented with 2 hours of sharp left-sided chest pain, which improved after being given aspirin and nitroglycerin. Her past medical history includes pernicious anemia, osteoporosis, and cataract.

The patient's diagnosis was non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and she underwent cardiac catheterization with drug-eluting stent placement in the right coronary artery (RCA) and left circumflex (LCX) arteries. Her medications at discharge included atorvastatin 80 mg, clopidogrel 75 mg daily, and aspirin lifelong, as well as metoprolol and nitroglycerin as needed.

The patient's physical examination was notable for a regular rhythm, normal s1 and s2, and a II/VI systolic murmur best heard at the right upper sternal border. Her laboratory results showed elevated troponin-T levels, with a peak value of 0.37. Her echocardiogram revealed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved regional and global biventricular systolic function, mild-moderate mitral regurgitation, and mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension.

The patient's urine culture grew Escherichia coli and Corynebacterium species, and her electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with LVH and new T wave inversion in aVL. Her chest X-ray showed mild cardiomegaly with no congestion or edema.

The patient's past medical history includes osteoarthritis of her lower spine, asthma, cluster migraines, and left renal cell carcinoma. She has also had a history of migraines, which were treated with sumatriptan. 

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a rigid bronchoscopy with cryotherapy and electrocautery to open her airways and improve her breathing. After the procedure, she developed shortness of breath and likely had a blockage of her airway from a mucous plug or blood clot. She was admitted to the ICU for non-invasive ventilation and respiratory monitoring, and her breathing improved overnight. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of renal cell carcinoma with metastases and post-procedure respiratory distress secondary to airway plugging. She was prescribed several medications, including albuterol, ipratropium bromide, levothyroxine, omeprazole, sunitinib, and Tylenol Extra Strength. She was also advised to follow up in clinic in one month with a CT scan of her airways.

Discharge Instructions:
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 

site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills.  
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 

cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 

home.
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon or your primary physician.
6. Please keep your wounds clean. You may shower starting five 
days after surgery, but no tub baths or swimming for at least 
four weeks. No dressing is needed if wound continues to be 
non-draining. Any stitches or staples that need to be removed 
will be taken out by the visiting nurse or rehab facility two 
weeks after your surgery.
7. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 

follow-up appointment in four weeks.
8. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve, 
motrin, etc).
9. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your lovenox for three weeks 

to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  After 
completing the lovenox, please take Aspirin 325mg  TWICE  daily 
for an additional three weeks.
10. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four week 

checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound each 
day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. Check 
wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or thick 
yellow drainage. 
Staples will be removed by the visiting nurse or rehab facility 
in two weeks.
11. ___ (once at home): Home ___, dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks, and staple removal at two weeks after 
surgery.
12. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. CPM as tolerated. No strenuous exercise or heavy 
lifting until follow up appointment.  Mobilize

The patient underwent a right total knee replacement procedure, which was uncomplicated and she tolerated well. The patient's discharge medications include oxycodone for pain, enoxaparin for DVT prophylaxis, docusate sodium for constipation, aspirin for DVT prophylaxis, senna for constipation, multivitamin, ferrous sulfate, fluticasone-salmeterol, albuterol sulfate, clonazepam, losartan, lamotrigine, metoprolol succinate, citalopram, and simvastatin.

The patient was discharged to home with services in stable condition, with instructions to follow up with her primary physician and to return to the emergency department or notify her physician if she experiences any complications.

What You ___ Experience:
 You may experience headaches and incisional pain.

noticeable on the second and third day of surgery.  You apply

swelling. The swelling will be its worse in the morning after

the surgery and will improve with time. Softer foods may be 
easier during this time.

answer: The patient is an 88-year-old female who was admitted to the hospital due to hematochezia. She has a history of gastric cancer, hypertension, and dementia. She was previously treated for a bleeding vessel at an anastomosis site and underwent clipping of the gastroduodenal artery. On admission, she had a hemoglobin level of 33 and was given a blood transfusion. She underwent endoscopy and colonoscopy, which showed a large amount of clot in the stomach and an ulceration at the anastomotic site. The patient was given medications including Pantoprazole, Acetaminophen, Lidocaine Jelly, Ondansetron, and Metronidazole. She was also given 2 liters of normal saline and had a nasogastric tube placed. The patient's vital signs were stable, and she was able to ambulate without difficulty. The patient's urine culture showed the presence of beta streptococcus group B and Escherichia coli. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of upper gastrointestinal bleed and was advised to follow her hematocrit levels closely and continue taking proton pump inhibitors and carafate slurry.

answer: The patient is an 88-year-old male who was admitted with bradycardia and complete heart block. He presented to his primary care physician with decreased energy and was found to have a heart rate of 39 beats per minute. He was transferred to the hospital for temporary transcutaneous pacemaker placement due to complete heart block with a wide ventricular escape at 35 beats per minute. The patient has a past medical history of hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, dementia, coronary artery bypass grafting, cerebrovascular accident, congestive heart failure, depression, and hypothyroidism. He also has a family history of unknown heart problems in his father and mother who died of pneumonia. The patient's physical examination revealed a well-nourished male in no acute distress, with a normal mental status and no signs of chest pain, shortness of breath, or abdominal pain. Laboratory results showed elevated creatinine and potassium levels, as well as a slightly elevated white blood cell count. Imaging studies revealed chronic ischemic changes in the left occipital lobe, as well as calcifications in the carotid siphons and vertebrobasilar system. The patient underwent an echocardiogram, which showed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function, as well as mild aortic and mitral regurgitation. The patient was discharged with a pacemaker in place and prescribed medications for his condition.
